title: The Council of the Malignant
subtitle: At first light they bind Jesus and lead Him to His death

stillness: Be still now, at the hour of Prime.
With a sorrowing and grieving heart, take up this scene.
There is no haste in love; sit a while with the bound Christ.
gospel: When morning came, as soon as it was day,
the chief priests and the elders of the people
and held a council against Jesus, to deliver Him to death.
And they said: If you are the Christ, tell us.
He answered: If I tell you, you will not believe me;
but from this time the Son of Man shall be seated
at the right hand of the power of God.
Then they all said: You then are the Son of God?
And He said: You say that I am.
And they said: What further testimony do we need?
For we ourselves have heard it from His own mouth.
And they led Him, bound, from Caiaphas to Pilate.
Then Judas, seeing that He was condemned, repented,
and brought back the thirty pieces of silver, saying:
I have sinned, betraying innocent blood.
behold: Behold, and attend to each thing as if you were present.
See how at once, as soon as it was day, they gathered against Him.
This is the assembly of the malignant, whose abbot is Caiaphas.
And when they would lead Him from the house, they say to Him:
Jesus, what are you doing? Shall we go, or not?
Come, for the priests command that you be led to the council,
where they await you with the people, to hand you over to Pilate, that you may die.
And you, if you still think yourself to be with Him,
say with great compassion: Alas, my Lord! Alas, good Master!
for now they wish to ruin you, now they wish to thrust you to death.
O Lord, what shall I, wretched one, do?
O how cruel, how sorrowful these tidings!
O Lord, what a mournful sight your most sweet Mother will have,
what bitter rumours she will hear, and all who love you with her!
See then how they lead Him as a gentle lamb borne to the slaughter,
and how He enters bound and afflicted, and all gaze upon Him,
and insulting Him they say: O good Jesus, is it you?
consider: Consider now how greatly the Jews thirsted for the blood of Jesus Christ,
who at earliest morning sought that sentence of death be given against Him.
For so great was their wickedness that no delay should be set,
lest He be handed to Pilate to be killed without pause.
By their own sentence they condemn themselves,
who deliver Him to death whom by witness of mouth and work they know to be God.
Consider with what shame Christ is led through the midst of Jerusalem,
the chief priests and Pharisees reviling Him, saying:
Come with us, you robber, you deceiver of the people; now your crimes shall soon have their end.
Horrible impiety indeed, which could not be sated with so many injuries,
but rather, raging with savage madness, exposed the soul of the just One
to the wicked judge, as to a mad dog, to be devoured.
And Judas, led by repentance, yet fruitless, for it was without hope of pardon,
was sorrowful not for the love of God, but for the enormity of the deed.
Behold truth shining on every side: the betrayer gives testimony of himself,
and stops the mouths of those who condemned Him.
prayer: who at the first hour of the day suffered yourself to be led, bound, into the council of the Jews,
and after many injuries and revilings to be led from Caiaphas to Pilate,
where you were judged by the Jews a man guilty of death and a malefactor,
and were falsely accused of many things;
and then to be sent from Pilate to Herod, and there accused and reckoned a fool,
and despised, and mocked in a white garment,
and at last, so mocked and clothed in white, sent back to Pilate:
grant me, I beseech you, to bear injuries and revilings patiently and gladly for the glory of your name,
and to appear before your face with joy,
and ever to gaze upon your desirable countenance. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Horrenda prorsus impietas, quæ tantis injuriis satiari non potuit, quin potius ferali rabie fremens, impio judici, tamquam rabido cani, animam justi deglutiendam exposuit.
latinJewel.english: Horrible impiety indeed, which could not be sated with so many injuries, but rather, raging with savage madness, exposed the soul of the just One to the wicked judge, as to a mad dog, to be devoured.
